does: This paper identifies and addresses the thinking-answer gap in multimodal reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for large vision-language models. Through analysis of rollouts during Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training, the authors demonstrate that semantic inconsistencies persist between reasoning processes and final answers in both training and inference phases. They propose CORA, a plug-and-play consistency reward model that introduces thinking-answer semantic alignment into RLVR training. The method further incorporates Hybrid Reward Advantage Splitting (HRAS) to stably coordinate optimization between task performance and consistency objectives, enabling more reliable multimodal reasoning that maintains coherence from intermediate steps to final outputs.
connects: This work extends recent advances in RLVR for large language models into the multimodal domain, building on approaches like GRPO that have shown success in eliciting reasoning capabilities through reinforcement learning with verifiable correctness signals. Prior multimodal RLVR research has concentrated on improving visual coverage in reasoning traces and reducing visual hallucinations, treating these as the primary failure modes. CORA responds to a gap in this literature by shifting focus to semantic consistency between reasoning and answers—a dimension largely overlooked by existing methods. The work also builds on reward modeling and multi-objective optimization techniques that balance competing training signals, adapting these approaches to the specific challenge of aligning intermediate reasoning with final predictions in vision-language models.
enables: This research opens directions for more robust multimodal reasoning systems where intermediate steps reliably support final conclusions. The plug-and-play consistency reward model could be adapted to other multimodal RLVR frameworks beyond GRPO or extended to other vision-language tasks requiring faithful reasoning. The HRAS mechanism for coordinating multiple reward signals has broader applications in multi-objective RL for language and vision models. By establishing thinking-answer consistency as a measurable and trainable objective, this work enables future research into interpretability and trust in multimodal reasoning, potentially leading to systems where users can better understand and verify the logical flow from visual observations through reasoning to conclusions.
